cataloging_subject.label: The Lansdowne MS of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloging unit is Frederick J. Furnivall's seven-part Chaucer Society edition-level compilation represented by University of Michigan item AGZ8236.0001.001. Its principal sequence prints the Canterbury Tales and the collateral Tale of Gamelyn in the order of British Library Lansdowne MS 851, but it also incorporates explicitly marked readings and passages from twelve other English manuscripts, uses Thomas Tyrwhitt's paragraph divisions in Melibee and the Parson's Tale, quotes Middle French comparison passages from Royal MS 19 C XI, adds five source-specific textual appendices and a Christ Church Nun's Priest end-link, and advertises or includes visual material copied from the Ellesmere and Cambridge Gg.4.27 manuscripts. This record does not collapse that mixed editorial object into the abstract Canterbury Tales, Lansdowne MS 851 alone, any supplemental witness, the separately issued physical parts, the U-M OCR/TEI encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.date: Canterbury Tales composed c. 1387-1400; bound title-page range 1868-1879; seven parts issued 1868-1884

The Lansdowne MS of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
notes: Capitalization is normalized from display capitals in the source edition.

titles.value: The Lansdowne ms of Chaucer's Canterbury tales / edited by Frederick J. Furnivall.

agents.name: Anonymous author of The Tale of Gamelyn
notes: The edition labels Gamelyn spurious in relation to Chaucer's Canterbury Tales; this record does not assign it to Chaucer.

agents.name: Thomas Tyrwhitt
notes: Furnivall states that he retained Tyrwhitt's paragraph divisions for Melibee and the Parson's Tale because those divisions were not present in Lansdowne MS 851.

agents.name: William Harcourt Hooper
notes: The edition credits Hooper with cutting the Ellesmere-derived drawings on wood. The controlled responsibility vocabulary has no wood-engraver role, so that credit is recorded on the visual-apparatus part rather than forced into an inaccurate textual role.

scope.description: The eleven sequence containers representing the Canterbury Tales core; this excludes Gamelyn, source-specific alternative appendices, and visual apparatus.
Assembly of the Lansdowne-order text, marked passage supplies, textual appendices, and visual apparatus into this edition-level compilation.
Manuscript-specific texts printed in the edition; exact witness and passage scopes remain in the lineage manifest.

assertion.notes: The edition presents the manuscript text under Furnivall's editorship, but the checked evidence does not identify every person involved in transcription, proofing, or production.

scope.description: Paragraph segmentation retained for Melibee and the Parson's Tale, not source text for the whole edition.

date_statements.display: Bound-volume title-page range 1868-1879; seven parts issued from 1868 through 1884
notes: The normalized interval preserves both the title-page imprint range and separately issued part dates; it is not a medieval composition date.

date_statements.display: The Canterbury Tales was begun probably in the late 1380s and remained unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400

scope.description: Chaucerian Canterbury Tales material only; not Gamelyn, the edition, or visual apparatus.
The principal manuscript witness for this edition's sequence and readings, not a composition date for either work.

places.label: Gloucestershire-Worcestershire-Herefordshire border area
notes: This is the linguistic association in the official Lansdowne manuscript record, not a claim that Chaucer composed the abstract Canterbury Tales there.

scope.description: Publication place of the nineteenth-century edition.
Linguistic association of the principal witness as cataloged by the British Library.
Nineteenth-century editorial titles, notices, headings, and apparatus.
Rubrics, quotations, and embedded passages rather than the primary language of the edition.

form_statements.description: The edition combines verse tales and links, two prose tales, prose editorial matter, and non-textual visual apparatus.

scope.description: The twenty-two conventional verse tales, collateral Gamelyn, and verse textual appendices; prologues and links carry their own part-level form.
Serious and parodic constituent romances; Gamelyn remains unclassified pending dedicated scholarship.

subject_statements.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

summaries.value: Furnivall's Lansdowne edition presents the General Prologue and twenty-four conventional Canterbury tales in the manuscript's A-B alpha-F(Squire)-D-E-F(Franklin)-G-C-B beta-H-I order, with the non-Chaucerian Tale of Gamelyn embedded after the Cook's Tale. It is an editorially composite manuscript edition: marked readings and passages come from twelve additional English witnesses, a lost Lansdowne leaf in the Parson's Tale is supplied from Royal MS 18 C II, Melibee and the Parson's Tale retain Tyrwhitt's paragraph divisions, and separate textual and visual apparatus extends beyond the principal witness.

The principal sequence contains eleven manuscript-order groups and twenty-four conventional tales. Gamelyn is collateral and contributes zero to that count. A Christ Church Nun's Priest end-link appears on p. 700; appendix pp. 1*-8* print five alternative or supplemental links and passages. Fourteen Ellesmere-derived teller cuts are present in the checked Part III carrier, while a twenty-three-image Ellesmere suite and a twelve-figure Gg.4.27 suite are advertised in the appendix contents. Two complete scans confirm both full suites in Furnivall's later 1885 Harley MS 7334 carrier; only whether and where they accompanied the earlier Lansdowne issue remains unresolved.

extent.description: Twenty-four conventional Canterbury tales are represented in the principal sequence, and the 69 content parts expose its groups, tales, links, prologues, commentary, appendices, and visual subparts. Gamelyn is collateral and contributes zero to the item and headline verse totals. The XML itself contains 55 DIV2 units, 622 LG elements, and 18,361 L elements; twenty-five L elements occur inside notes, leaving 18,336 direct non-note L elements. Gamelyn contributes 2 LG and 906 direct L, leaving the headline 620 verse groups and 17,430 direct line elements in the Canterbury Tales and edition matter. The XML also contains 215 direct P elements and 756 PB milestones. These are encoding-representation metrics, not a new critical lineation and not a claim that prose serialized in P is verse.

content_parts.label: Teller portraits and announced manuscript drawings
content_parts.summary: Fourteen Ellesmere-derived teller woodcuts are present in the checked Part III carrier. Appendix contents advertise a twenty-three-teller Ellesmere suite and six teller plus six allegorical figures from Cambridge Gg.4.27. Two complete scans prove both advertised suites were issued in Furnivall's later 1885 Harley MS 7334 volume; their exact earlier Lansdowne-edition carrier history remains unresolved.

content_parts.summary: The Lansdowne appendix announces six teller designs and six allegorical figures from Gg.4.27. They were not found in the seven checked Lansdowne BSB carriers, but both complete suites occur in two independently inspected 1885 Harley edition scans.

related_works.title: The Canterbury Tales
notes: The edition contains a witness-ordered, passage-level composite representation of the Canterbury Tales rather than a homogeneous Lansdowne transcription or a claim about Chaucer's final intended order.

related_works.title: The Tale of Gamelyn
notes: The edition labels Gamelyn spurious in relation to Chaucer and it is not counted among the twenty-four conventional Canterbury tales.

evidence.citation: Structural and semantic inspection of pinned agz8236 XML

evidence.summary: Verifies the eleven-group A-B alpha-F(Squire)-D-E-F(Franklin)-G-C-B beta-H-I principal sequence, twenty-four conventional tale count, collateral four-line link plus 902-line Gamelyn, group/page boundaries, exact marked witness supplies, p. 700 Christ Church link, appendix topology, 18,336 direct non-note L elements, 622 LG, 215 direct P, and 756 PB milestones.

evidence.citation: General Corpus reviewed metadata manifest for The Canterbury Tales
evidence.summary: Supplies evidence-backed abstract-work authorship, composition range, cultural origin, form, genre, subjects, and twenty-four-tale context reused only at the explicitly scoped Canterbury Tales parts.

Provides full entity, relation, passage scope, access, cache, rights, editorial-practice, and unresolved-question evidence for the repository XML, U-M encoding, seven-part immediate edition, principal and supplemental witnesses, Tyrwhitt segmentation, Thomson study, and manuscript facsimiles.

open_questions.question: How should each separately issued Chaucer Society part, its printed date, the bound title-page range, later reprints, and copy-specific binding arrangement be crosswalked bibliographically?
next_steps: Collate additional complete copies and Chaucer Society issue lists against the seven exact BSB carriers.
Preserve intellectual appendix order separately from physical binding order and later reprint dates.

open_questions.question: Where, and in which issue states, did the complete advertised twenty-three-image Ellesmere suite and twelve-figure Gg.4.27 suite accompany the earlier Lansdowne edition?
next_steps: Compare additional Lansdowne-edition part and bound-volume carriers with later Chaucer Society carriers that preserve the complete suites.
Retain the Gg.4.27 relation as an announcement or citation for this Lansdowne edition until exact issue-specific carrier evidence is documented.
notes: The seven checked BSB Lansdowne carriers contain fourteen Ellesmere-derived cuts and the appendix announcements, but no located copy of the complete advertised suites.

open_questions.question: What composition date, dialect region, and preferred current genre description should be assigned specifically to this Tale of Gamelyn text?
next_steps: Consult a current critical edition or dedicated scholarly record for Gamelyn rather than transferring the date or dialect of Lansdowne MS 851 to the abstract work.

open_questions.question: How should the edition's printed lineation, Six-Text references, raw XML L/LG structure, prose serialization, and modern canonical Canterbury Tales numbering be crosswalked?
next_steps: Retain the current reproducible representation metrics without presenting them as a new critical lineation.
Build a passage-level concordance against a current critical edition before normalizing line identifiers.

notes: The sequence A-B alpha-F(Squire)-D-E-F(Franklin)-G-C-B beta-H-I is Furnivall's manuscript-centered order for this edition; it is not silently generalized as Chaucer's final authorial ordering.
Lansdowne remains the principal source even though marked passage supplies make several groups composite and one lost Parson leaf is supplied from Royal MS 18 C II.

Gamelyn is collateral edition content, not a twenty-fifth Chaucerian Canterbury tale. Its 906 direct L elements are included in the edition-level representation total but excluded from the twenty-four-tale count.

The brief Middle French Royal MS 19 C XI comparison material is supporting apparatus, not a substantive language facet of the cataloged compilation; its language and function remain scoped in lineage.

The complete Ellesmere and Gg.4.27 manuscript bundles are cached as supporting sources, while metadata and lineage locators point only to the exact discontiguous leaves relevant to the edition's actual or advertised visual apparatus.
Rights, public access, cache presence, exact download links, request routes, and provider terms are recorded per entity and access route in the linked lineage manifest.
